audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/packemon-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-iulhf08i...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-iulhf08i
Analyzing packemon-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-iulhf08i) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources that are fetched from the project's official GitHub repository and GitHub's release infrastructure. The `source` array retrieves the LICENSE and README files from the raw content URL of the specific version tag (`v${pkgver}`), while the architecture-specific `.deb` packages are pulled directly from the GitHub Releases page. All URLs are constructed using the known-safe `_urlraw` and `url` variables that point to `https://github.com/ddddddO/packemon` and its raw content subdomain.

The sourcing of this PKGBUILD during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will execute only the top-level variable assignments and expansions, which involve no command substitution or external code execution. The `_pkgver`, `_pkgname`, and URL variables are statically defined without any use of `$(...)`, backticks, or other command injection mechanisms. There is no `pkgver()` function, so no dynamic version computation occurs. The `sha256sums` are explicitly listed and match the expected sources.

Fetching these sources via `makepkg --printsrcinfo` and during the source download phase is safe, as it involves only standard HTTPS requests to GitHub's trusted domains, and no code is executed during parsing. The use of `SKIP` is not present, and all sources have proper checksums. There is no evidence of data exfiltration or remote code execution during the sourcing phase.
</details>
<summary>
Safe source retrieval from trusted GitHub repository
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe source retrieval from trusted GitHub repository[/]

LLM auditresponse for README-1.8.25:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a README for the `packemon-bin` package, version 1.8.25. It contains documentation describing the functionality, features, installation methods, and use cases of Packémon, a TUI tool for generating and monitoring network packets. The content is purely informational and includes usage examples, feature lists, and setup instructions.

There is no executable code in this file that poses a security risk. The installation instructions reference standard and legitimate sources (GitHub releases, Homebrew, AUR, Go install) and do not include obfuscated commands, suspicious downloads, or unauthorized remote code execution. Commands using `curl | bash`, `sudo`, or `go install` are presented in a standard, transparent manner consistent with software documentation.

While the tool uses raw sockets and eBPF (noted in the documentation), this is disclosed as part of its intended functionality for packet manipulation and is not hidden or malicious. The README even includes warnings about experimental features and potential bugs.

No evidence of supply-chain attacks, data exfiltration, backdoors, or malicious behavior is present in the file. The content aligns with standard open-source project documentation.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation only, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed README-1.8.25. Status: SAFE -- Documentation only, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves a prebuilt .deb package from the project's official GitHub repository release page for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures. The source URLs are constructed predictably using tagged release versions (v${pkgver}), which is standard practice for binary packages. Checksums for all sources, including architecture-specific binaries, are provided and verified via sha256sums arrays—no 'SKIP' is used, indicating strong integrity enforcement.

The package() function extracts the contents of data.tar.gz (which is part of the extracted .deb) into the pkgdir using tar. This behavior is typical when repackaging .deb archives into Arch-compatible packages. The LICENSE and README files are also sourced from the same release tag via raw.githubusercontent.com, with proper checksums included.

No suspicious commands (e.g., curl|bash, eval, base64 decoding, git pull at build time) are present. All network fetches are from the project's own trusted upstream (github.com/${_pkgauthor}/${_pkgname}), and no data exfiltration or remote execution mechanisms appear. The operations align with standard AUR packaging practices for binary distribution.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package build from verified sources
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary package build from verified sources

LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `.nvchecker.toml` file is a configuration for nvchecker, a tool used to monitor upstream version changes for software packages. The configuration specifies that the version for `packemon-bin` should be tracked from the GitHub repository `ddddddO/packemon`, using the latest release tag with a `v` prefix. This is a standard and legitimate use of nvchecker, commonly found in AUR packages to automate version updates.

There are no executable commands, network operations beyond version checking, or file manipulations in this file. It does not perform downloads, execute code, or exfiltrate data. The source is clearly defined and matches the expected upstream for the package. No obfuscation or suspicious behavior is present.

This configuration solely supports version tracking and poses no security risk. It is consistent with normal AUR maintenance workflows and contains no malicious elements.
</details>
<summary>
Valid version-tracking config, no security issues
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/4] Reviewing LICENSE-1.8.25, src/control, src/md5sums...

LLM auditresponse for control:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a `control` file typically found within Debian packages, containing metadata about the package such as name, version, maintainer, and description. No executable code, scripts, or commands are present in this file. The content is plain text and consists solely of package metadata, including a descriptive blurb for Packémon, a TUI tool for packet monitoring and transmission.

There are no signs of malicious activity, obfuscation, or unauthorized behavior. The homepage points to a legitimate GitHub repository (github.com/ddddddO/packemon), and no network operations, file manipulations, or system calls are encoded or implied in this file. All fields conform to standard Debian control file syntax and semantics.

While the email address in the Maintainer field uses a seemingly obfuscated format ("lbfdeatq@gmail.com"), this does not constitute a security threat in itself, especially in the context of a metadata-only file. No payloads, exploits, or suspicious directives are present.
</details>
<summary>
Metadata-only file, no executable content or threats
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/4] Reviewing LICENSE-1.8.25, src/md5sums...
+ Reviewed src/control. Status: SAFE -- Metadata-only file, no executable content or threats
LLM auditresponse for md5sums:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file provided is a `md5sums` file commonly used in Arch Linux AUR packages to store checksums of bundled files. It contains a single entry: an MD5 hash for the file `usr/bin/packemon`. This is a standard practice for verifying the integrity of prebuilt binaries in `-bin` packages. The presence of a valid, unaltered checksum entry indicates an attempt to ensure file authenticity.

There is no executable code, network activity, or system modification instructions in this file. It is a passive declaration used by the packaging system and poses no direct security risk. While MD5 is cryptographically weak and not recommended for security-critical applications, its use here for basic integrity checking in a trusted packaging workflow is consistent with common AUR practices, especially in `-bin` packages where upstream binaries are distributed as-is.

The file does not exhibit any signs of malicious content such as encoded payloads, suspicious URIs, or commands. It aligns with expected packaging behavior and does not deviate into dangerous or unexpected operations.
</details>
<summary>
Standard checksum file, no malicious content detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

[3/4] Reviewing LICENSE-1.8.25...

It includes a valid copyright notice for "Ochi Daiki" (2024) and contains no executable code, scripts, or any form of logic that could pose a security risk. The text outlines redistribution terms and disclaimers of warranty and liability, consistent with common open-source licensing practices.

There are no network operations, file manipulations, obfuscated content, or potentially harmful commands present. The use of `"` is simply HTML entity encoding for quotation marks and does not indicate any malicious intent or code injection. This file serves only a legal and informational purpose, typical in software packages.

As such, this file presents no evidence of supply-chain attacks, backdoors, or malicious behavior. It aligns fully with expected packaging practices and contains only benign, human-readable license text.
</details>
